content:
A successful maintenance command does not mean PostgreSQL was allowed to reclaim the tuples that matter.

6 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

VACUUM can finish successfully and leave the table looking almost exactly as sick as before.
That is not a contradiction. It is PostgreSQL obeying visibility rules.
The dangerous assumption is that a completed vacuum equals completed cleanup. It does not. A vacuum can scan pages, update statistics, do useful freezing work, and still be unable to remove row versions that an old snapshot may need.
So when dead tuples keep climbing, running VACUUM harder is not a diagnosis. It is another maintenance job competing for I/O while the actual blocker remains untouched.
The useful question is not, “Did vacuum run?”
It is, “What was vacuum legally allowed to remove?”
VACUUM does not delete whatever looks old
PostgreSQL uses multiversion concurrency control. An UPDATE usually creates a new row version. A DELETE marks a version as no longer current. Those old versions cannot simply disappear the moment a newer transaction stops seeing them.
Another transaction may still hold a snapshot under which those versions are visible.
That is why PostgreSQL calls them tuples, not disposable debris. Their status depends on which transactions might still need them. VACUUM only reclaims a dead tuple when it is no longer visible to any relevant transaction.
The official PostgreSQL documentation states the basic rule plainly: updated or deleted tuples are not physically removed immediately; they remain until vacuum can reclaim them. It also explicitly tells operators dealing with old transaction IDs to find long-running transactions using age(backend_xid) or age(backend_xmin).
That distinction explains a common dashboard argument:
→ The operations view says autovacuum ran.
→ The table view says dead tuples remain.
→ Both views can be correct.
VACUUM also has several jobs. It can reclaim reusable space, update the visibility map, freeze old tuples to protect against transaction ID wraparound, clean indexes, and update planner statistics when combined with ANALYZE.
A run can make progress on some of those jobs while making little progress on the row versions you care about.
There is another subtle point. Standard VACUUM usually makes reclaimed space available for reuse inside the table. It generally does not return that space to the operating system. Watching the filesystem and expecting the relation file to shrink is therefore the wrong test.
VACUUM FULL can rewrite and compact a table, but it takes an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock and needs extra disk space for the rewrite. It is not the emergency button to press because a graph looks ugly.
The oldest snapshot sets the cleanup boundary
The transaction that matters most may not be doing visible work.
It may be idle in transaction.
It may be a forgotten SQL console, a worker that opened a transaction before waiting on a queue, a connection returned incorrectly to a pool, or an application path that began a transaction and then performed a slow network call.
The query finished. The transaction did not.
Start with the sessions, not the vacuum command:
SELECT
pid,
usename,
application_name,
client_addr,
state,
xact_start,
query_start,
age(backend_xid) AS xid_age,
age(backend_xmin) AS xmin_age,
wait_event_type,
wait_event,
left(query, 160) AS query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE backend_xid IS NOT NULL
OR backend_xmin IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY xact_start NULLS LAST;Do not terminate the first old-looking process automatically. Identify the owner, workload, transaction state, and rollback consequence first.
An old active transaction may be processing a legitimate batch. An old idle transaction is usually more suspicious, but killing it still discards uncommitted work.
Replication can hold the line too. A physical replication slot can retain WAL when a consumer falls behind. A logical slot can retain both WAL and catalog-related state. Hot standby feedback can tell the primary that a replica still needs row versions, preventing cleanup on the primary to avoid query conflicts on the standby.
Inspect the replication side:
SELECT
slot_name,
slot_type,
active,
restart_lsn,
confirmed_flush_lsn,
xmin,
catalog_xmin
FROM pg_replication_slots;Then inspect replicas and their lag:
SELECT
application_name,
client_addr,
state,
sent_lsn,
write_lsn,
flush_lsn,
replay_lsn,
replay_lag
FROM pg_stat_replication;These are different retention mechanisms. Do not collapse every vacuum problem into “long query.” The blocker can be a transaction, a prepared transaction, replication feedback, or a slot whose consumer has disappeared.
Your dead-tuple graph is an estimate
pg_stat_user_tables.n_dead_tup is useful. It is not a forensic count.
Statistics are estimates derived from PostgreSQL’s statistics subsystem. They can lag, reset, or differ from what a page-level inspection would find. Treat the value as a trend and trigger, not a courtroom exhibit.
Use it alongside vacuum timing and table activity:
SELECT
relname,
n_live_tup,
n_dead_tup,
n_mod_since_analyze,
last_vacuum,
last_autovacuum,
vacuum_count,
autovacuum_count
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC
LIMIT 20;Then check whether a vacuum is currently running and what phase it has reached:
SELECT
p.pid,
p.datname,
p.relid::regclass AS table_name,
p.phase,
p.heap_blks_total,
p.heap_blks_scanned,
p.heap_blks_vacuumed,
p.index_vacuum_count,
a.xact_start,
a.wait_event_type,
a.wait_event
FROM pg_stat_progress_vacuum AS p
JOIN pg_stat_activity AS a USING (pid);Progress views answer “What is this worker doing?” They do not prove that every candidate tuple was removable.
Also separate dead tuples from table bloat. Dead versions can cause bloat, but relation size alone does not tell you how much space is reusable, how it is distributed across pages, or whether indexes carry their own excess. A large table that reuses free space efficiently may be stable. A smaller hot table with constant churn may hurt more.
The operational signal is a combination:
→ dead-tuple trend
→ update and delete rate
→ autovacuum frequency and duration
→ oldest transaction and snapshot age
→ table and index growth
→ query latency and buffer activity
One graph cannot carry that argument alone.
Tune autovacuum after removing the handcuffs
Autovacuum wakes based partly on a threshold and a fraction of table size. The familiar shape is:
autovacuum_vacuum_threshold
+ autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor × estimated table rowsThat default-style model can be too relaxed for a very large, heavily updated table. A small percentage of a huge table is still a huge number of dead tuples.
Per-table settings can make sense:
ALTER TABLE orders SET (
autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.02,
autovacuum_vacuum_threshold = 1000,
autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.01
);Those numbers are examples, not universal recommendations. Choose them from the table’s write rate, acceptable dead-tuple budget, vacuum duration, and available I/O.
Do not tune first if an old snapshot is blocking removal. More frequent workers do not overrule MVCC. Faster workers arrive at the same legal boundary sooner.
Likewise, raising worker counts without considering I/O and memory can move the bottleneck. Lowering cost delays can help a vacuum finish sooner, but it can also make foreground latency worse. Increasing maintenance_work_mem may improve some maintenance work, but multiplied across concurrent workers it becomes a capacity decision.
The sequence matters:

• Confirm the dead-tuple trend is real enough to investigate.

• Find old transactions, prepared transactions, slots, and standby feedback.

• Resolve or intentionally accept the retention source.

• Observe another vacuum cycle.

• Tune thresholds and worker capacity for recurrence prevention.
That order keeps configuration changes attached to evidence.
Build a cleanup policy, not a VACUUM ritual
The durable fix is usually outside the database command itself.
Put a ceiling on accidental transaction lifetime. PostgreSQL provides idle_in_transaction_session_timeout specifically for sessions that sit idle inside an open transaction. Set it with care because terminating such sessions can surprise badly behaved applications, but leaving them immortal is not safer.
Keep external calls outside database transactions where correctness allows. Make pool behavior observable. Give every client a meaningful application_name. Alert on transaction age and replication-slot retention before disk growth becomes the first symptom.
And document what can be terminated, by whom, after which checks.
For the live commands, decision paths, and PostgreSQL failure patterns behind lock contention, connection exhaustion, replication lag, and vacuum pressure, use PostgreSQL War Room: 10 Fix Patterns for Lock Contention, Connection Exhaustion & Missing Indexes. This is the thing. It is built for the moment the maintenance graph and production latency disagree.
This article follows PostgreSQL’s official guidance on routine vacuuming, the VACUUM command, and the PostgreSQL monitoring views. Verify behavior against the version you operate because available columns and vacuum behavior evolve.
If you want to test whether you can recognize database and infrastructure failure modes without memorizing syntax, Froquiz has 10,000+ questions plus scenario-based Senior Dev Challenges across SQL, Docker, Git, AWS, microservices, and more.
→ Froquiz
When dead rows keep winning, stop scheduling rematches. Find the snapshot holding the referee.
